title: Electrosurgical Burn Injuries and Their Prevention
abstract:
Electrosurgical Burn Injuries
and Their Prevention
Charles G. Battig, MD
Electrocution hazards associated with electrical mon-
itoring
devices
have recently
received
attention.
The
recurring problem of accidental electrosurgical burns has
received less publicity. Most such burns are caused by
faulty attachment of the patient ground plate. This plate
provides
a low-current-density pathway
for the high-
frequency cautery current. Failure of this pathway results
in burns at body areas in contact with other fortuitous
ground points. Battery-clamp types of connectors
are
most unreliable and should not be used to attach the
ground plate. Built-in, ground-circuit monitors may warn
of a failure in the connection to the ground plate but not
of failure of contact between patient and ground plate.
Especially serious burns can result from failure of the
ground circuit if a patient is connected to other electrical
devices in addition to the electrosurgical unit.
The
use of a multiplicity of electronic devices in
the monitoring of patients is becoming accepted
practice in a variety of patient care situations. Such
devices are now commonly used in the operating
room, recovery room, intensive care unit, coronary
care unit, and cardiac catheterization laboratory.
Recently, reports in the literature have drawn atten¬
tion to the electrical shock hazards to the patient,
resulting from improper connection of the equip¬
ment.1'4 The potential benefits of electronic devices
can be tragically negated by disregarding the fun¬
damentals of proper interconnection, grounding, and
elimination of stray current paths.
It is not necessary to look to new and exotic
electronic instruments for these potential hazards.
The electrosurgical unit is a familiar sight in most
operating rooms. Yet, aside from its incompatibility
with explosive anesthetic agents, little attention has
recently been given to its hazards, as pointed out
in the review by Mitchell and Lumb.5 Improper
connection between the unit and the patient ground
plate, or between the ground plate and the patient
can result in serious burns to the patient. Such
accidents
are usually preventable. The following
review of the principles of the electrosurgical unit
is presented with the aim of providing a working
understanding of its safe usage.
Electrosurgical Principles
Tissue-heating properties of radio-frequency cur¬
rents were studied and described by many early
investigators including d'Arsonval (1893),6 Thomp¬
son
(1889),
Pozzi
(1907),
and
Nagelschmidt
(1909).' The patient ground plate (indifferent elec¬
trode)
was introduced by Iredell and Turner in
1919.8 Early systems used Oudin or Tesla coil res¬
onators which resembled the spark-gap radio trans¬
mitters of the day. These spark-gap oscillators, de¬
signed
to produce
recurrent pulses
of damped
oscillations,
were studied by Ward (1925)
who
established their usefulness for coagulation pur¬
poses.9 The subsequent introduction of the elec¬
tronic vacuum tube permitted construction of radio-
frequency oscillators with a pure, undamped output.
Studies by Ward also showed the efficacy of the
pure
sinusoidal
current
for cutting
purposes.9
Electrosurgery
was enthusiastically incorporated
into the neurosurgical armamentarium by Harvey
Cushing, who used
a unit specially designed by
Bovie (1928).10 Equal enthusiasm accompanied the
introduction of the technique into general surgery,
especially for cancer surgery, by Clark (1926).1'
In 1929, McLean presented a discussion of elec¬
trosurgical theory.12 He included
a technical de-

lic Health Service, US Department of Health, Education, and

Downloaded From: http://jama.jamanetwork.com/ by a New York University User  on 05/19/2015
 scription by Bovie and presented the results of a
variety of histological studies of the effects of dif¬
ferent types of current wave forms on tissue. A
series of refinements have been made in commercial
units over the subsequent years. However, the basic
combination of the spark-gap generator and a vacu¬
um-tube oscillator has been retained in most surgi¬
cal models. Coagulation is usually accomplished
with the spark-gap generated wave form, while the
cutting current is provided by a vacuum-tube os¬
cillator.
The spark-gap, radio-frequency generator differs
little from the device Marconi first used to transmit
radio waves. Components are selected to produce a
current output consisting of pulses of damped sine
waves (Fig 1, top). The specific frequencies em¬
ployed are not critical and have been chosen on an
empirical basis. Typical units operate in the range
from 1 to 3 megacycles per second. The operation of
this type of generator is relatively unaffected by its
load impedance. Thus, it tends to produce a satis¬
factory coagulation effect from one patient to the
next without critical readjustments of the circuit.
The damped sinusoidal output of this generator
produces tissue coagulation by its localized heating
effect
on
tissues
as
described by
Ward9
and
McLean.12
Properly functioning vacuum-tube oscillators pro¬
duce a continuous single frequency sine wave, with
no damping
(Fig
1, bottom).
The undamped,
single-frequency output of the vacuum-tube oscil¬
lator is used primarily for cutting purposes, but it
can also be employed for coagulation.12 Tissue cut¬
ting occurs not as the result of a sharp edge on the
cautery tip; it is attributed to dissolution of the
molecular structure of the tissue cells in the im¬
mediate path of the electrode by the electrical arc.
Reliable functioning of vacuum-tube oscillators is
somewhat dependent upon proper electrical imped¬
ance matching between the output circuit and the
patient. Some adjustment of the tuning circuit may
therefore be necessary to compensate for differences
in electrical impedance among patients and elec¬
trodes, though most modern units are designed to
eliminate this requirement.
The fact that all electrosurgical devices are basi¬
cally radio-frequency generators becomes distress¬
ingly apparent when attempts are made to use sensi¬
tive electronic devices in the vicinity of an active
cautery. From an electrical-equipment interference
point of view, the spark-gap generator produces an
undesirable output, consisting of radio-frequency
energy scattered over a wide range of frequencies
extending from 120 cycles to over 3 megacycles per
second. The high level of power radiated by the unit
can completely overwhelm neighboring monitoring
and recording devices and render them intermittent¬
ly useless. This occurrence is familiar to anyone who
has observed electrocardiogram and electroenceph¬
alogram recorders during the periods when the unit
is in use. A need exists for an electrosurgical device
which will not cause such severe interference with
1. Top, Train of damped sine waves produced by gen¬
erator. Bottom, Steady-state sine wave.
2. Cautery ground plate provides relatively large sur¬
face area for body contact. Resultant current density is
too low to cause burn. Significant heating occurs at
cautery tip only.
the sensitive systems for patient monitoring now
in use or planned for many operating suites.
Radio-frequency currents generated by the elec¬
trosurgical unit behave in some respects like static
electric charges. For example, they tend to be dis¬
tributed toward the surface of
an object rather
than uniformly throughout. In addition, they con¬
centrate at sharp bends or on surface irregularities.
Practical use is made of these properties in the de¬
sign of the electrocautery tip. Although available in
a variety of shapes, the electrodes
are usually
pointed or have a thin cross section. High current
densities are attained at the points and provide the
intense localized power responsible for the tissue
effects (Fig 2).
The patient ground plate provides a low-current-
density path to complete the electrosurgical circuit.
In contrast to the effect at the cautery tip, the
purpose here is to minimize tissue heating. It is
difficult to specify the exact size requirement for a
ground plate. Mitchell and Lumb suggest a maxi¬
mum safe power density of approximately 1.5 w/sq
cm.5 Most operating-room units have power outputs
in the range of 100 to 300 w, and the standard plate
currently
supplied
with
these
units
measures
23 X 36 cm. If a "worse case" circumstance is as¬
sumed wherein the entire 300 w were dissipated

uniformly and entirely within the ground plate, a

be reached. A plate of this size therefore provides a
reasonable safety factor when in actual use. Large
plates are desirable because they can spread the
current over a large area of the body. However, the
important factor is the actual surface area in con¬
tact with the patient, rather than the overall di¬
Causes and Prevention of Injuries
For most general surgical procedures, the ground
plate can be placed under the thighs or the lower
portion of the buttocks of the patient. Bony areas
of the body, such as the sacral, tibial, pelvic, and
scapular regions should be avoided in order to pre¬
vent pressure necrosis or burns. Mitchell and Lumb
suggest that placement of the ground plate, rela¬
tive to the operative site, should not enable current
to pass through the axis of the heart.5 There seems
to be little reason to observe this precaution, which
is, in fact, usually ignored in practice. However, a
situation in which such placement would be of ut¬
most importance is in patients having implanted
artificial cardiac pacemakers.13
Lead and stainless steel are commonly used ma¬
terials for ground plates. Lead has the advantage
of sufficient flexibility to conform to the patient's
contours, but it corrodes rapidly and tends to fa¬
tigue with repeated flexings. Surface irregularities
which result from repeated use can become areas of
increased pressure and current flow, resulting in
tissue burns" (Fig 3). Such ridges in the lead
should always be smoothed out between
dures. Wraparound bandaging is sometimes used to
affix the lead ground plate to a patient's extremity.
This is potentially a dangerous practice, since if the
bandaging is too tight it can cause neurovascular
injury. Stainless steel plates have the advantages of
being easy to clean, being resistant to electrolytic
corrosion, and possessing excellent mechanical sta¬
The connection between the ground wire and the
indifferent electrode is extremely important to pa¬
tient safety. This connection, if not a permanent
nector. Ground-wire connections made with battery
or alligator clamps (Fig 4) are extremely hazardous
and should be eliminated. Especially dangerous are
such clamps with no protective rubber boot. Clamp
connectors can be easily dislodged from the ground
plate during surgery. Unnoticed, these free clamps
can cause serious burns to adjacent body surfaces
because of their small area of contact with the skin
and high resultant tissue current density. A par¬
ticularly desirable junction consists of
wire terminated with an insulated female connector
which is threaded so that it fits onto a male con¬
Should the connector become detached, it will not
cause a burn at its site of patient contact, since the
active end of the ground wire is recessed in its in¬
Because of the importance of a proper patient-
grounding circuit, alarm systems have been built to
warn of a break in the ground plate circuit.5 A
typical system is shown in Fig 6. Two ground wires
connect to the ground plate. The wires are insulated
from each other along their length, but are elec¬
trically connected via the ground plate itself, form¬
ing a closed electrical circuit. The electrosurgical
unit circulates a small 60-cps current through this
ground circuit, this current can no longer flow, and
an internal alarm sounds and deactivates the unit.
Although the ground circuit from the generator to
the ground plate is monitored, absolutely no indi¬
cation of adequate contact of the patient with the
ground plate is afforded by this system. Skin burns
can still occur, even though the alarm indicates a
satisfactory ground plate circuit, if there is inade¬
quate contact between the patient and the ground
Electrosurgical units equipped with
monitoring circuit may occasionally cause 60-cps in¬
terference with cardioscopes or other similar moni¬
toring equipment connected to the same patient.
This interference is not a direct result of the high-
frequency current. It is continuous in nature and
5. Screw-type connection to ground plate. Cut-away
drawing shows insulated housing in which is recessed
disappears only when the unit is completely turned
off or disconnected from the supply mains. The
source of the interference is the low-level, 60-cps
current which continuously circulates through the
ground circuit to monitor its intactness. Some of
this current may flow through the patient's body
from the ground plate to the ECG leads. Usually, a
proper ground connection between the cardioscope
and the unit will eliminate this interference. If
this does not solve the problem, the following tech¬
nique is suggested: After the usual amount of elec¬
trolyte jelly is applied to the ground plate, it is
wrapped with a single sheet of thin plastic food
wrap. Additional electrolyte jelly is then applied to
the upper surface of the ground plate over the plas¬
tic film, and the plate is positioned under the pa¬
tient in the usual manner. This thin plastic film and
metal plate form a capacitor which blocks the low-
frequency, 60-cps current from entering the patient
and interfering with the attached monitoring equip¬
ment. It also provides the patient a measure of pro¬
tection from shock should a component failure occur
within the ground-monitoring circuit itself. The
cautery current passes this capacitor with little at¬
tenuation so that the operation of the machine is
Some form of electrolyte is recommended to lower
skin resistance and provide a more intimate contact
between the skin and ground electrode. Surgical
lubricating jelly, electrode paste, and strong sodium
chloride solutions all provide skin moisture and en¬
hance the conductive path to ground. Bland prep¬
arations should be used, because the patient may
lie on such substances for many hours. These sub¬
stances dry out over a period of time and become
less efficient conductors. Primary reliance must,
therefore, be placed on proper skin-to-electrode con¬
tact—not on the electrolyte substances alone. Liq¬
uid electrolytes, such as 25% sodium chloride solu¬
tion, are used to saturate toweling wrapped about
the ground plate. Such wrappings must be smooth
lest the weight of the patient resting on wrinkles in
the fabric produce pressure necrosis or a thermal
The ground plate is designed to be the preferen¬
tial ground path for the high-frequency current.
By avoiding contact of the patient with metal parts
of the operating table, it is possible to enhance this
function. Practically, however, this is most difficult
to observe because of other conductive paths pro-
6. Ground plate monitoring circuit. Flow of monitoring
current is through twin-conductor ground wire (arrows).
Break in ground wire or in its ground plate contact will
interrupt monitoring-current flow and sound alarm.
7. Connection of other grounded electrical apparatus to
patient may cause burns should cautery ground fail.
vided by spillage of blood or saline solution. Should
the ground become defective, current will follow any
other available low-resistance path from the patient
to ground. The seriousness of the resulting thermal
injury will depend upon the area of contact and
current density achieved. Such burns can affect the
surgeon or anesthesiologist as well as the patient.10
The sacral area is a common site of burns for pa¬
tients. Many operating-table mattresses have de¬
tachable lower portions with a central opening for
urological procedures. Often the mattress will pull
apart enough during other operative procedures so
that the patient makes contact with the metal op¬
erating table. Should the cautery ground system
fail, the radio-frequency currents can flow to ground
through this portion of the metal table. Blood
circulation through the sacral area is inadequate to
conduct away the heat generated in the tissues by
the cautery ground current. The resulting burns
are slow to heal and can be quite distressing to the
In a given situation, the behavior of the current
generated by the electrosurgical unit may appear
ground, the radio-frequency currents will flow to
ground through whatever point or points provide
the lowest impedance pathways. At radio frequen¬
cies, stray capacitive effects become quite important
in determining these paths. It is difficult to
ually inspect the physical layout of an electrosurgi¬
cal system and accurately predict all potential sites
of burn injury. Duplication of the circumstances
associated with an accidental burn is also quite
difficult. Small changes in the physical placement
of each component, ambient humidity, and contact
resistance can all combine to produce
which is entirely different from the original. The
provision of a proper ground circuit will prove much
more rewarding than the task of reconstructing the
events leading to a burn injury.
Current patient-monitoring practice often
quires the use of EEG and ECG electrodes.2 This
is a particularly dangerous situation when the elec¬
trosurgical unit is also in use. Safe engineering
practice calls for the joining to
point the individual grounds of all electrical devices
connected to a patient. This procedure prevents the
development of potential differences between the
patient and various pieces of electrical equipment
should a component failure occur. While protecting
the patient from an electrical shock hazard, this
grounding procedure can cause serious burns from
high-frequency currents. Should the cautery ground
circuit fail, the monitoring electrodes may function
as alternate ground points. Because of the small
size of the electrodes, significant current densities
can occur at skin contact sites and produce deep
burns (Fig 7). Insertion of fuses
quency chokes in the monitoring leads has been
suggested but is not entirely reliable. Battery-op¬
erated devices have also been recommended but are
not commercially available or in common use, except
for the cystoscope light. Since little can be done to
alter such patient-machine interconnections, the
hazard, and the provision of a reliable and adequate
ground-plate circuit at all times.
Often the surgeon will call for increasing the
cautery output to make up for a weak coagulation
or cutting action. Whether the electrosurgical unit
has or does not have an internal, ground circuit,
warning system, the operator should suspect a pos¬
sible defect whenever the normal power control set¬
a grossly inadequate tissue effect.
Such warning systems provide no assurance of an
adequate contact between the ground plate and the
patient, thus the ground circuit must be checked
whenever cautery action is unsatisfactory. For pro¬
cedures performed on children, the cautery should
always be started at minimum output and gradual¬
ly increased to operating level.
Although not directly related to burn hazards,
there are two situations involving the use of the
electrosurgical unit which are of clinical interest.
While performing urological procedures, the unini¬
tiated surgeon may receive a forceful kick from his
patient if he employs the cautery in the vicinity of
the obturator nerve. Nerve stimulation should be
recognized as causing the patient's activity, rather
than an insufficient depth of anesthesia. The anes¬
thesiologist can judiciously use small amounts of a
short-acting muscle relaxant if use of the cautery
Occasionally, quite vigorous muscle contractions
may accompany the use of the cautery over large
muscle masses. Gross movement of the patient may
be a real hazard. If reducing the cautery current
does not suffice, small amounts of a short-acting
muscle relaxant can be administered. The relaxant
will not necessarily completely abolish all move¬
ment. Muscle fibers can still respond to the direct
electrical stimulation of the cautery even though
the motor end plates are blocked.
An informed interest in the proper application of
the electrosurgical unit to the patient
prevent needless injury. Attention to
functioning ground circuit will eliminate the cause
for the majority of accidental burns.
